Should genetic screening of embryos be limited to preventing serious medical conditions, or should parents have broader freedom to select traits?

students.name: Mira
students.text: I believe genetic screening should be strictly limited to serious medical conditions. Allowing parents to select non-medical traits would create a society where children are designed to meet parental preferences rather than accepted for who they are. This could deepen social inequality, as wealthier families would have access to genetic advantages that others cannot afford. We risk reducing human reproduction to a consumer transaction, which fundamentally changes how we value human life.

students.name: Tyler
students.text: I think parents should have broader freedom in genetic screening because restricting reproductive choices sets a dangerous precedent for government control over family decisions. Parents already make countless choices that shape their children's futures, from nutrition to education. If the technology exists to give a child the best possible start in life, it seems arbitrary to allow screening for some conditions but not others. Clear ethical guidelines can prevent extreme abuses without eliminating parental choice entirely.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As cities expand and traffic grows more congested, transportation planners debate the best long-term solution for urban mobility. Some advocate for investing heavily in autonomous vehicle technology, arguing that self-driving cars will reduce accidents and optimize traffic flow. Others believe that cities should instead invest in expanding rail and bus rapid transit systems, which have a proven track record. Which investment do you think will have the greater impact on urban transportation over the next twenty years?

students.name: Chloe
students.text: I believe autonomous vehicles will have the greater impact because they can transform existing road infrastructure without massive construction projects. Self-driving cars can communicate with each other to reduce congestion, eliminate human error that causes most accidents, and provide door-to-door service that public transit cannot match. As the technology matures and costs decrease, autonomous vehicles will become accessible to most urban residents and fundamentally change how cities manage transportation.

students.name: Kofi
students.text: Public transit investment will have a far greater impact because autonomous cars still take up road space and contribute to congestion. A single bus can replace fifty individual cars on the road, and rail systems move thousands of people per hour more efficiently than any car-based technology. Cities with excellent public transit like Tokyo and Singapore already demonstrate that trains and buses are the most effective solution for dense urban areas. Autonomous vehicles are a technological distraction from proven infrastructure solutions.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The fashion industry is the second-largest polluter globally, and the rise of fast fashion has accelerated environmental damage through overproduction and textile waste. Some designers advocate for a return to slow fashion, producing fewer but higher-quality garments at higher prices. Others argue that affordable clothing is a necessity and that the industry should focus on sustainable materials and circular production methods instead. What approach do you think is most realistic for making the fashion industry more sustainable?

students.name: Zara
students.text: Slow fashion is the most realistic path to sustainability because it addresses the root problem of overconsumption. When people invest in well-made clothing that lasts years instead of buying cheap items they discard after a few wears, total textile waste decreases dramatically. Brands like Patagonia have shown that consumers will pay more for durable, ethically produced clothing. We need to shift the cultural mindset from treating clothes as disposable to valuing craftsmanship and longevity.

students.name: Andre
students.text: Slow fashion is unrealistic because many people simply cannot afford expensive clothing. Telling low-income families to buy fewer, pricier garments ignores economic reality. A more practical approach is requiring fast fashion companies to use recycled materials, implement take-back programs, and invest in textile recycling technology. This allows affordable clothing to remain available while reducing the environmental footprint of production. Sustainability should not be a luxury that only wealthy consumers can participate in.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The use of animals in scientific research remains one of the most debated topics in both science and ethics. While animal testing has contributed to major medical breakthroughs, including vaccines and surgical techniques, new alternatives like organ-on-a-chip technology and computer modeling are becoming increasingly sophisticated. Some scientists argue that animal testing is still irreplaceable for understanding complex biological systems, while others believe we have reached a point where alternatives can largely replace animal models. Should scientific institutions set firm timelines for phasing out animal testing?

students.name: Suki
students.text: Yes, setting firm timelines would accelerate the development and adoption of alternative methods. Without deadlines, institutions have little incentive to invest in new technologies when established animal testing protocols already exist. The European Union's push to reduce animal testing has spurred remarkable innovation in computational biology and tissue engineering. A phased timeline gives researchers time to adapt while sending a clear signal that the scientific community takes animal welfare seriously.

students.name: Nathan
students.text: Firm timelines could be dangerous for scientific progress and public health. Some areas of research, particularly drug safety testing and immunology, still require whole-organism studies that alternatives cannot fully replicate. Rushing to eliminate animal models before replacements are validated could lead to unsafe medications reaching patients. I think institutions should aggressively fund alternative methods and reduce animal use wherever possible, but arbitrary deadlines risk compromising the quality of research that saves human lives.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Archaeological excavation methods have evolved significantly over the past century. Traditional approaches involve physically digging through layers of soil, carefully documenting each artifact's position. However, modern technologies like ground-penetrating radar and LIDAR allow researchers to map buried structures without disturbing the ground at all. Some archaeologists argue that non-invasive methods should become the default approach, while others believe that physical excavation remains essential for a complete understanding of a site. What do you think is the better primary approach for modern archaeology?

students.name: Soren
students.text: I believe non-invasive technologies should be the primary method. Every time we dig, we permanently alter or destroy parts of the archaeological record. Future generations may have even better tools to analyze sites we haven't touched. Using remote sensing first lets us make smarter decisions about where and whether to excavate at all.

students.name: Adaeze
students.text: While technology is impressive, I think physical excavation must remain central to archaeology. Remote sensing can show us shapes and structures underground, but it cannot reveal the textures, organic materials, or subtle details that only hands-on work uncovers. Many of our greatest discoveries came from direct contact with artifacts in their original context.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The pharmaceutical industry faces ongoing debate about drug pricing. Brand-name medications are often extremely expensive because companies invest billions in research, clinical trials, and regulatory approval. Generic drugs offer the same active ingredients at a fraction of the cost but can only be produced after patents expire. Some argue that patent periods should be shortened to make medications affordable sooner, while others say strong patent protection is necessary to incentivize innovation. Should pharmaceutical patent periods be reduced to improve public access to affordable medicine?

students.name: Beatriz
students.text: I strongly support shorter patent periods. People around the world suffer and even die because they cannot afford essential medications. If generic versions could enter the market sooner, prices would drop dramatically. The current system prioritizes corporate profits over human lives, and that is a moral failure we need to address urgently.

students.name: Henrik
students.text: Shortening patents sounds compassionate, but it could backfire. Developing a new drug costs an average of two billion dollars, and companies need time to recoup that investment. Without strong patent protection, pharmaceutical firms would have less incentive to fund risky research into new treatments. We could end up with cheaper drugs today but fewer breakthroughs tomorrow.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Forest management strategies differ widely depending on priorities. Some foresters advocate for selective logging, where only certain trees are harvested while the rest of the ecosystem remains intact. Others support clear-cutting designated areas followed by replanting, arguing it is more efficient and can actually benefit certain species that thrive in open environments. Both methods have economic and ecological implications. In your view, which timber harvesting approach is more sustainable for long-term forest health?

students.name: Tariq
students.text: Selective logging is clearly the more sustainable option for long-term forest management. It preserves biodiversity by maintaining the forest canopy and protecting habitat for countless species. Clear-cutting devastates ecosystems, causes soil erosion, and disrupts water cycles. Even with replanting, it takes decades for a clear-cut area to resemble a mature forest again.

students.name: Astrid
students.text: I think clear-cutting gets an unfairly bad reputation. When managed responsibly with proper replanting programs, it can actually create diverse habitats. Some tree species and wildlife depend on open, sunlit areas that only exist after clearing. Selective logging can also damage forests through repeated equipment access and trail creation over many years.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Video game designers constantly balance artistic vision with player expectations. Some developers create games with extremely high difficulty levels, arguing that overcoming tough challenges provides a deeper sense of accomplishment. Others believe games should include adjustable difficulty settings so that a wider audience can enjoy the experience regardless of skill level. This tension has sparked heated discussion in the gaming community. Should game designers prioritize accessibility by including difficulty options, or is maintaining a single intended difficulty level a valid artistic choice?

students.name: Bodhi
students.text: Games are an art form, and artists should have the freedom to design the experience they envision. A single difficulty level ensures every player shares the same journey and emotional arc. Adding easy modes can undermine the game's core message. Films do not offer simplified plots for casual viewers, and games should not compromise their vision either.

students.name: Ines
students.text: I disagree. Including difficulty options does not diminish artistic vision; it expands who can appreciate it. Players with disabilities, limited time, or less gaming experience deserve to enjoy these stories and worlds too. Accessibility features in other media, like subtitles or audio descriptions, are celebrated. Games should embrace the same inclusive philosophy.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Housing affordability is a pressing concern in many cities worldwide. One proposed solution is rent control, which limits how much landlords can increase rent each year. Supporters say it protects tenants from being priced out of their neighborhoods. Critics argue that rent control discourages new construction and reduces the overall housing supply, ultimately making the problem worse. Based on what we have discussed about housing markets, do you think rent control is an effective policy for addressing housing affordability?

students.name: Rohan
students.text: Rent control provides immediate relief to vulnerable tenants who would otherwise face displacement. In rapidly gentrifying neighborhoods, it is often the only thing keeping communities intact. While it may not solve every housing problem, protecting existing residents from sudden, dramatic rent increases is a legitimate and necessary policy goal that markets alone cannot achieve.

students.name: Maeve
students.text: Rent control treats the symptom rather than the disease. Economic research consistently shows that it reduces housing supply over time because developers and landlords have less incentive to build or maintain properties. Instead of capping rents, cities should focus on zoning reform and incentives for new construction to increase the total housing stock available.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The rise of citizen journalism through social media has transformed how news is reported and consumed. Ordinary people with smartphones can now document events in real time, sometimes reaching audiences before professional journalists arrive on the scene. However, citizen journalists typically lack formal training in verification, ethics, and balanced reporting. Some media scholars welcome this democratization of news, while others worry about the spread of misinformation. Do you believe citizen journalism strengthens or weakens the quality of public information?

students.name: Lucian
students.text: Citizen journalism is a powerful force for accountability. Professional media outlets have their own biases and blind spots, and they cannot be everywhere at once. When ordinary people share footage of police misconduct or natural disasters, they fill critical gaps in coverage. The public benefits from having more sources of information, not fewer.

students.name: Amara
students.text: The problem with citizen journalism is that anyone can publish anything without verification. Professional journalists follow ethical codes, check sources, and provide context. Citizen reports often lack these safeguards, leading to rumors and misinformation spreading rapidly. The public may feel more informed, but the quality of that information is frequently unreliable.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Classical dance forms like ballet have strict technical standards that dancers must master through years of rigorous training. Contemporary dance, by contrast, often values individual expression and innovation over established technique. Some dance educators believe that all serious dancers should first build a strong foundation in classical technique before exploring contemporary styles. Others argue that requiring classical training can limit creativity and exclude dancers whose bodies or backgrounds do not fit traditional molds. Should classical training be considered essential for all professional dancers?

students.name: Nia
students.text: Classical training provides discipline, strength, and body awareness that benefit any style of dance. Learning ballet fundamentals gives dancers a shared vocabulary and physical control that translates across genres. Even the most innovative contemporary choreographers often have classical backgrounds. Starting with strong technique does not limit creativity; it gives dancers more tools to express themselves.

students.name: Idris
students.text: Requiring classical training as a foundation is an outdated and exclusionary idea. Many brilliant dancers come from hip-hop, folk, or cultural traditions that have their own rigorous techniques. Insisting on ballet as the starting point centers one particular Western tradition and marginalizes others. Dance education should honor multiple pathways to excellence.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The nursing profession is experiencing a significant staffing shortage in many countries. One debated solution is increasing the use of travel nurses, who accept temporary assignments at hospitals facing shortages, often at much higher pay than permanent staff. Hospitals benefit from immediate staffing relief, but permanent nurses sometimes feel undervalued when temporary colleagues earn substantially more for similar work. This can affect morale and retention. Is the widespread use of travel nurses a good solution to the nursing shortage?

students.name: Dalia
students.text: Travel nursing addresses urgent needs that cannot wait for long-term solutions. When hospitals are dangerously understaffed, patient safety is at risk. Travel nurses step in quickly and bring diverse experience from working at multiple facilities. The higher pay reflects the personal sacrifices of constant relocation and the market demand for their flexibility.

students.name: Callum
students.text: Relying on travel nurses is a short-sighted approach that actually worsens the shortage. When permanent staff see travel nurses earning double or triple their salary, many quit to become travelers themselves. This creates a destructive cycle. Hospitals should invest that money in raising permanent staff wages and improving working conditions to retain their core nursing teams.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Global supply chains have proven vulnerable to disruption, as seen during recent pandemics and geopolitical conflicts. Many companies are now considering reshoring, which means bringing manufacturing back to their home countries rather than relying on distant overseas factories. Reshoring can reduce shipping delays and political risks but typically increases production costs. Some experts advocate for a middle ground called nearshoring, moving production to nearby countries instead. Should companies prioritize reshoring their supply chains, even at higher cost?

students.name: Petra
students.text: Reshoring is worth the higher cost because supply chain resilience is priceless. The disruptions we have witnessed showed how fragile global networks can be. Having production closer to home means faster response times, better quality control, and reduced dependence on politically unstable regions. Companies should view reshoring as a long-term strategic investment rather than a short-term expense.

students.name: Mateo
students.text: Completely reshoring is impractical for most companies. The cost difference is simply too large, and consumers are not willing to pay significantly higher prices. A smarter approach is diversifying suppliers across multiple countries and regions. This reduces risk without abandoning the economic advantages of global trade. Total reshoring would raise prices and could even slow economic growth.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Plastic has become one of the most versatile and widely used materials in modern life, but its environmental impact is severe. Researchers are developing biodegradable alternatives made from plant-based materials like corn starch or seaweed. However, these bioplastics currently cost more to produce, sometimes perform worse than traditional plastics, and may require specific industrial conditions to decompose properly. Should governments mandate a transition from conventional plastics to biodegradable alternatives, even given current limitations?

students.name: Leila
students.text: We cannot wait for perfect alternatives before acting. Plastic pollution is devastating our oceans and ecosystems right now. Government mandates would accelerate investment in bioplastic research, driving costs down and performance up over time. The initial limitations are growing pains that will be resolved much faster with regulatory pressure pushing the industry forward.

students.name: Nikolai
students.text: Mandating a switch before bioplastics are truly ready could cause more harm than good. If biodegradable plastics do not decompose properly outside industrial facilities, we just create a different pollution problem. A better approach is funding research and offering incentives for development while improving recycling infrastructure for existing plastics in the meantime.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Deep-sea mining has emerged as a potential way to extract valuable minerals like cobalt, nickel, and manganese from the ocean floor. These minerals are essential for batteries and renewable energy technologies. However, marine scientists warn that mining could cause irreversible damage to fragile deep-sea ecosystems that we barely understand. The deep ocean hosts unique species and plays important roles in carbon cycling. Should deep-sea mining be permitted to support the transition to renewable energy, or should the ocean floor remain off-limits?

students.name: Freya
students.text: The deep ocean is Earth's last truly unexplored frontier, and we should not destroy it before we even understand it. Deep-sea ecosystems recover incredibly slowly, if at all. We should invest in recycling existing minerals, developing alternative battery technologies, and mining responsibly on land rather than opening up an entirely new environment to industrial extraction.

students.name: Darius
students.text: The green energy transition requires enormous quantities of specific minerals, and land-based mining alone cannot meet that demand. Deep-sea mining, if carefully regulated, could provide these critical resources with a smaller surface footprint than terrestrial mines. Blocking it entirely could actually slow the shift away from fossil fuels, which poses a far greater environmental threat.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: When addressing homelessness, communities generally pursue one of two broad strategies. The housing-first model provides homeless individuals with permanent housing immediately, without requiring them to meet conditions like sobriety or employment first. The traditional approach requires individuals to complete treatment programs and demonstrate readiness before receiving housing. Housing-first has shown promising results in some cities, but critics question whether it addresses root causes of homelessness. Which approach do you think is more effective for reducing homelessness long-term?

students.name: Tessa
students.text: Housing-first is clearly more effective because it recognizes that stability is a prerequisite for recovery, not a reward for it. Research shows that people are far more likely to address addiction, mental health, and employment challenges when they have a safe, permanent place to live. Requiring readiness first keeps people trapped in a cycle of shelters and streets.

students.name: Joaquin
students.text: Housing-first sounds ideal, but without addressing the underlying issues that caused homelessness, we risk simply relocating problems rather than solving them. Some individuals need structured treatment programs to develop the skills necessary for maintaining housing independently. Providing housing without adequate support services can lead to property damage, eviction, and a return to homelessness.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Fossil specimens are invaluable scientific resources, yet many end up in private collections after being discovered on private land. In some countries, fossils found on private property legally belong to the landowner, who may sell them to the highest bidder. Scientists argue that important specimens should be accessible for research in public institutions. Landowners counter that they should benefit financially from discoveries on their own property. Should governments require that scientifically significant fossils be placed in public museums rather than sold to private collectors?

students.name: Thandi
students.text: Scientifically significant fossils belong to all of humanity, not just whoever owns the land they are found on. Once a rare specimen disappears into a private collection, it is lost to science forever. Governments should require that major fossil finds be deposited in public institutions where researchers and the public can study and appreciate them.

students.name: Felix
students.text: Forcing landowners to surrender fossils without fair compensation would actually discourage people from reporting discoveries. Many important finds are made by amateur collectors and farmers who invest time and resources in extraction. If the government can simply take their finds, people will hide or ignore specimens. A better approach is offering fair purchase prices and tax incentives.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: The rapid growth of short-term rental platforms like Airbnb has disrupted the traditional hotel industry and transformed neighborhoods in tourist destinations. Supporters say these platforms provide travelers with affordable, authentic accommodation options while allowing homeowners to earn extra income. Critics argue that short-term rentals reduce available housing for local residents, drive up rents, and disturb residential neighborhoods. Should cities impose strict regulations on short-term rental platforms to protect housing availability and neighborhood character?

students.name: Yara
students.text: Cities absolutely need to regulate short-term rentals strictly. In popular tourist destinations, entire apartment buildings have been converted into informal hotels, displacing long-term residents and hollowing out communities. Local families should not have to compete with tourists for housing. Reasonable regulations like limiting the number of rental days per year can balance tourism income with residents' needs.

students.name: Finn
students.text: Heavy regulation stifles economic opportunity and consumer choice. Many hosts are ordinary people renting out a spare room to supplement their income, not corporate landlords. Strict rules often punish small-scale hosts while doing little to address larger housing affordability issues. Cities should focus on building more housing rather than restricting how people use their own property.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As robots and artificial intelligence become more capable, they are increasingly being used in caregiving roles, particularly for elderly individuals and people with disabilities. Robotic caregivers can assist with physical tasks, medication reminders, and even basic companionship. Proponents argue that robots can help address the global shortage of human caregivers. However, some ethicists worry that replacing human connection with machine interaction could harm the emotional well-being of vulnerable people. Is it ethical to use robots as primary caregivers for elderly or disabled individuals?

students.name: Karim
students.text: Robotic caregivers should supplement human care, not replace it. The caregiver shortage is real, and robots can handle physical tasks like lifting patients or dispensing medication reliably. This frees human caregivers to focus on emotional support and complex decision-making. Used thoughtfully, robots can actually improve the quality of care that vulnerable people receive.

students.name: Sienna
students.text: Relying on robots for caregiving is a deeply troubling path for society to take. Elderly and disabled individuals often suffer most from loneliness and isolation. A machine cannot provide genuine empathy, comfort, or meaningful conversation. Normalizing robotic care risks sending the message that these vulnerable populations do not deserve real human attention and connection.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: DNA databases maintained by law enforcement have become powerful tools for solving crimes, including cold cases that went unsolved for decades. Some countries are expanding these databases to include DNA samples from anyone arrested, not just those convicted of crimes. Advocates say broader databases increase the chances of identifying perpetrators. Civil liberties groups warn that collecting DNA from people who have not been convicted violates privacy rights and disproportionately affects certain communities. Should law enforcement be allowed to collect and retain DNA from arrested individuals who are not convicted?

students.name: Esme
students.text: Collecting DNA from arrested individuals goes too far. Being arrested does not mean someone is guilty, and retaining their genetic information permanently treats them as suspects for life. This practice disproportionately impacts communities that are already over-policed. DNA is deeply personal information, and the government should only retain it after a conviction, not merely an arrest.

students.name: Rashid
students.text: Expanded DNA databases save lives by solving crimes faster and even preventing future offenses. The process is no more invasive than fingerprinting, which has been standard practice for decades. If someone is truly innocent, having their DNA on file should not concern them. The potential to bring justice to victims and their families outweighs the minimal privacy intrusion.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Urban parks and green spaces are increasingly recognized as essential infrastructure, not just amenities. However, cities face a paradox: investing in new parks and improving existing ones often increases surrounding property values, which can displace the low-income residents who would benefit most from green space access. This phenomenon is sometimes called green gentrification. How should cities approach the development of urban green spaces while preventing displacement of vulnerable communities?

students.name: Vivian
students.text: Cities should pair green space investments with strong anti-displacement policies like affordable housing requirements and rent stabilization near new parks. The solution is not to avoid building parks in underserved neighborhoods but to protect existing residents while improving their environment. With proper planning, green infrastructure and social equity can coexist rather than conflict.

students.name: Oren
students.text: I think a more effective approach is distributing many small green interventions throughout neighborhoods rather than building large showcase parks. Smaller community gardens, green corridors, and pocket parks improve quality of life without attracting the kind of real estate speculation that drives gentrification. This decentralized strategy brings nature to people without transforming entire neighborhoods.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Artificial intelligence tools can now generate logos, layouts, and illustrations in seconds based on simple text prompts. Some designers embrace these tools as productivity boosters that handle routine tasks, freeing them to focus on higher-level creative strategy. Others fear that AI-generated design devalues the profession and threatens designers' livelihoods. Clients may increasingly question why they should pay for human designers when AI can produce acceptable results at a fraction of the cost. Will AI tools ultimately help or harm the graphic design profession?

students.name: Lena
students.text: AI will elevate the design profession, not destroy it. Just as desktop publishing did not eliminate designers in the 1990s, AI will shift the role toward more strategic and conceptual work. Designers who learn to use AI as a tool will become more productive and valuable. The profession will evolve, and those who adapt will thrive in this new landscape.

students.name: Kwesi
students.text: I am genuinely worried about the impact on designers, especially those starting their careers. Entry-level design work like creating simple logos or social media graphics is exactly what AI does best. If junior positions disappear, how will the next generation of designers gain experience? The profession could become increasingly elitist, accessible only to those who can afford years of unpaid creative development.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Nuclear power is experiencing renewed interest as countries seek carbon-free energy sources to combat climate change. Modern reactor designs are significantly safer than older ones, and nuclear plants produce enormous amounts of reliable, emissions-free electricity. However, concerns persist about radioactive waste storage, the high cost of construction, and the risk of catastrophic accidents, however unlikely. Some environmentalists now support nuclear energy, while others insist that solar and wind power alone can meet our needs. Should nuclear energy play a major role in the transition away from fossil fuels?

students.name: Sasha
students.text: Nuclear energy is essential for decarbonization. Solar and wind are intermittent and require massive energy storage solutions that do not yet exist at scale. Nuclear provides constant, reliable baseload power regardless of weather conditions. The waste issue is manageable with modern storage technology, and newer reactor designs have made meltdown scenarios virtually impossible. We cannot afford to exclude nuclear from our energy mix.

students.name: Juno
students.text: Nuclear power is too expensive, too slow to build, and too risky to be the answer. New nuclear plants take a decade or more to construct and cost billions more than projected. Meanwhile, solar and wind costs have plummeted and continue falling. Investing in renewable energy plus battery storage is faster, cheaper, and does not create radioactive waste that remains dangerous for thousands of years.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Major museums in Western countries hold millions of artifacts taken from other nations during colonial periods. In recent years, some institutions have begun returning objects to their countries of origin, acknowledging that these items were often acquired through force or exploitation. Others argue that museums serve as universal repositories of human heritage and that dispersing collections would reduce public access to important cultural objects. Should Western museums be required to return artifacts acquired during colonial eras to their countries of origin?

students.name: Aaliya
students.text: These artifacts were taken without consent during periods of exploitation and violence. Keeping them in Western museums perpetuates colonial power dynamics. Countries of origin have the right to their own cultural heritage, and many now have excellent museums capable of preserving and displaying these objects. Returning artifacts is not just a cultural act but a moral obligation and a step toward justice.

students.name: Conrad
students.text: While I understand the moral argument, returning every colonial-era artifact could be impractical and even harmful. Some originating countries lack the infrastructure to preserve fragile objects, and disputed ownership between modern nations complicates matters. Museums could instead pursue shared custody arrangements, rotating exhibitions, and digital access programs that honor cultural heritage while maintaining preservation standards.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: In recent decades, the use of technology in classrooms has expanded dramatically, with many schools now providing students with laptops or tablets. Some educators believe that technology enhances learning by providing access to vast resources and interactive tools. Others argue that it distracts students and reduces face-to-face interaction. Do you think that technology in the classroom is more beneficial or harmful to student learning? Why?

students.name: Amber
students.text: I believe technology in the classroom is more beneficial than harmful. When I was in high school, we used tablets for research and collaborative projects. It made learning more engaging and allowed us to access up-to-date information instantly. Technology also helps students with different learning styles, such as visual or auditory learners, to grasp concepts more easily. The key is to use it wisely and set clear guidelines to minimize distractions.

students.name: Mia
students.text: I think technology often does more harm than good in the classroom. In my experience, many students use laptops to browse social media or play games during lessons, which distracts them and those around them. Also, reliance on technology can weaken basic skills like handwriting and mental math. I believe that traditional methods, like direct instruction and printed books, foster deeper concentration and understanding.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: In recent decades, the number of electoral systems around the world has diversified significantly. Some countries use proportional representation, others use first-past-the-post, and some use a mix. What do you think is the most important factor in choosing an electoral system for a democratic country? Why?

students.name: Jonah
students.text: I believe the most important factor is ensuring fair representation for minority groups. In many first-past-the-post systems, smaller parties and minority voices are often ignored because they cannot win a majority in any single district. Proportional representation, on the other hand, allows parties that get a small percentage of the vote to still win seats. This makes the government more reflective of the entire population.

students.name: Jake
students.text: I think stability is the most crucial factor. A system that constantly produces coalition governments can lead to gridlock and frequent changes in policy. For instance, in some countries with proportional representation, governments collapse regularly. A first-past-the-post system usually gives one party a clear majority, which allows for decisive action and long-term planning. That stability is essential for a country's development.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Many countries have experienced a significant increase in the number of students pursuing higher education. This trend has led to debates about the most effective ways to support student success. Some argue that reducing class sizes is the key, while others believe that investing in technology and online resources is more beneficial. Which factor do you think is more important for improving student outcomes and why?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe that reducing class sizes is the more effective approach. Smaller classes allow teachers to give individual attention to each student, which can greatly improve learning outcomes. For example, when I was in high school, my math class had only 15 students, and the teacher could help us with specific problems. This personalized support helped me understand concepts better than in larger classes.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I think investing in technology and online resources is more important. With digital tools, students can access a wealth of information and learn at their own pace. For instance, online platforms provide interactive exercises and videos that cater to different learning styles. This flexibility can benefit a larger number of students than simply reducing class sizes, which may be costly and difficult to implement in many schools.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: In recent decades, the concept of 'digital nomadism' has gained popularity, with more people choosing to work remotely while traveling. This lifestyle offers flexibility and freedom, but it also raises questions about community and social ties. Do you think the rise of digital nomadism is a positive development for society, or does it undermine the importance of stable communities?

students.name: Jonah
students.text: I think digital nomadism is a positive development. It allows people to experience different cultures and broaden their perspectives, which can lead to more open-minded societies. Also, it reduces the need for commuting, which is good for the environment. While some worry about weak social ties, digital nomads often form strong online communities that can be just as supportive as physical ones.

students.name: Sarah
students.text: I disagree. Digital nomadism can be harmful because it weakens local communities. When people are constantly moving, they are less likely to participate in local events or build long-term relationships. This can lead to social isolation and a decline in community engagement. Stable communities are important for social support and civic participation, which are hard to maintain when everyone is transient.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: In recent decades, the rise of social media has changed how we interact with others. Some researchers argue that online social connections can be just as meaningful as face-to-face interactions, while others claim they lack depth. What do you think is the most significant psychological factor that determines whether an online relationship is fulfilling?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I think self-disclosure is key. When people share personal thoughts and feelings online, it builds trust and intimacy, just like in person. For example, I have a close friend I met in a gaming community. We talk daily about our lives, and I feel just as connected to her as to my offline friends. Without that openness, online interactions stay superficial.

students.name: Tim
students.text: I believe shared activities matter more. Doing something together, like playing a game or watching a stream, creates a sense of co-presence and bonding. In my experience, online relationships that involve regular joint activities feel more real than those based only on chatting. It is the shared experience that provides fulfillment.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Many countries have experienced a significant rise in the number of small businesses and startups in recent years. However, a large percentage of these new ventures fail within the first few years. Do you think that a formal business education, such as a degree in business administration, is essential for entrepreneurs to succeed?

students.name: Karen
students.text: I believe that a formal business education is not essential for entrepreneurial success. Many successful entrepreneurs, like Steve Jobs and Mark Zuckerberg, dropped out of college and built billion-dollar companies. What matters more is practical experience, creativity, and the ability to learn from failures. A degree might provide some theoretical knowledge, but it cannot replace real-world skills.

students.name: Amy
students.text: While it is true that some entrepreneurs succeed without a business degree, I think formal education provides a solid foundation that can prevent common mistakes. Courses in finance, marketing, and management teach essential skills that are difficult to learn on the job. Moreover, business schools offer networking opportunities and mentorship that can be invaluable for a new venture.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we are going to talk about ethics and technology. As artificial intelligence becomes more integrated into our daily lives, many philosophers debate whether AI systems can be held morally responsible for their actions. What do you think is the most significant ethical challenge posed by AI decision-making?

students.name: David
students.text: I think the biggest ethical challenge is accountability. When an AI makes a mistake, like a self-driving car causing an accident, it is unclear who is to blame: the programmer, the manufacturer, or the AI itself. Without clear responsibility, it is difficult to ensure justice for victims. This ambiguity undermines trust in AI systems.

students.name: Steve
students.text: While David makes a good point about accountability, I believe the most significant challenge is bias. AI systems often learn from historical data that contains human prejudices, leading to discriminatory outcomes in hiring, lending, or policing. Even if we assign responsibility, these biases can perpetuate social inequalities unless we actively address them.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Over the next few weeks, we are going to look at social inequality and its impact on educational opportunities. In many countries, children from low-income families tend to perform worse academically compared to their wealthier peers. Some argue that providing free school meals can help level the playing field by addressing hunger and improving concentration. Do you think governments should fund free school meals for all students, regardless of family income?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe free school meals should be available to all students. At my high school, the free meal program was only for low-income families, and many students who were just above the threshold went hungry. This created a stigma, and some kids refused to participate even if they qualified. A universal program would remove that stigma and ensure every child has access to nutritious food, which is essential for learning.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I hold a different view from Claire. While I agree that hunger affects learning, I think free meals should target only those in need. Offering them to everyone is an inefficient use of taxpayer money, especially when many families can afford to pay. In my cousin's school district, the universal program was so costly that they had to cut other programs like art and music. I think it is better to focus resources on the students who really need them.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about the preservation of historical sites. Many ancient monuments and buildings are at risk from natural disasters, urban development, and even tourism. Some argue that we should focus resources on preserving these sites for future generations, while others believe it is more important to invest in modern infrastructure and services. What do you think is the most significant factor to consider when deciding whether to preserve a historical site?

students.name: John
students.text: I think the most important factor is the cultural significance of the site. If a site represents a unique aspect of a civilization's history or identity, it should be preserved. For example, the ancient city of Pompeii gives us invaluable insights into Roman life. Losing such a site would be a great loss to humanity. So, cultural value should outweigh other considerations.

students.name: Sarah
students.text: I believe the cost and practicality of preservation are crucial. If a site is too expensive to maintain or if the money could be better spent on improving living conditions for people, then maybe we should let it go. For instance, preserving every old building in a city might not be feasible. Prioritizing modern needs like housing and hospitals sometimes makes more sense.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we discussed in class, communication in the digital age raises questions about the quality of social interactions. Some argue that constant connectivity through social media and messaging apps enhances relationships, while others believe it weakens face-to-face communication skills. Do you agree that digital communication has a negative impact on our ability to communicate in person? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe digital communication does weaken face-to-face skills. When we rely on texting and social media, we lose practice with nonverbal cues like tone and body language. I have noticed that my younger cousin, who spends a lot of time on her phone, struggles to maintain eye contact during conversations. It is important to balance online interaction with real-life communication to keep those skills sharp.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I disagree that digital communication has a negative impact. In fact, it can enhance our communication skills. Online platforms allow us to connect with diverse people and practice expressing ideas clearly and concisely. For example, I have made friends from other countries through online forums, and we have learned to communicate effectively despite language barriers. These experiences have improved my ability to adapt my communication style to different contexts.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we discussed in class, many countries are considering or have already implemented a four-day workweek to improve work-life balance and productivity. What do you think is the most significant benefit of a four-day workweek? Why?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe the most significant benefit is improved mental health. With an extra day off, employees have more time to rest, pursue hobbies, and spend time with family. This reduces stress and burnout, which can lead to higher job satisfaction and lower turnover rates. In my country, some companies that tried this reported happier employees and even increased productivity.

students.name: Paul
students.text: While Claire makes a good point about mental health, I think the most significant benefit is environmental sustainability. With one less commuting day per week, there would be fewer cars on the road, reducing carbon emissions and traffic congestion. This could have a substantial positive impact on the environment, especially in large cities. Additionally, offices would use less energy, further reducing the ecological footprint.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we discussed in class, many countries are considering or have implemented policies to lower the voting age to 16. Proponents argue it increases youth engagement and political participation, while opponents contend that 16-year-olds lack the maturity and life experience to make informed voting decisions. Which factor do you think is most important in this debate and why?

students.name: Mia
students.text: I believe lowering the voting age to 16 is a positive step. At 16, young people are already contributing to society through part-time jobs and paying taxes. They are also directly affected by policies on education, climate change, and gun control. Giving them a voice in elections would make them more engaged citizens and could lead to higher long-term voter turnout. We should trust them to make informed choices.

students.name: Helen
students.text: I think the voting age should remain at 18. At 16, many teenagers are still heavily influenced by their parents and teachers, and they may not have enough life experience to understand complex political issues. Moreover, they are not legally adults for other responsibilities like signing contracts or serving on juries. Lowering the age could lead to uninformed voting decisions that do not reflect their genuine preferences.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: In recent years, the concept of year-round schooling has gained attention as an alternative to the traditional long summer break. Proponents argue that it prevents learning loss and allows for more frequent breaks, while critics claim it disrupts family schedules and summer activities. Do you think primary schools should adopt a year-round calendar? Why or why not?

students.name: Ben
students.text: I believe year-round schooling would be beneficial. Research shows that students, especially from low-income families, lose significant knowledge over the summer. With shorter breaks, they retain more and teachers can spend less time reviewing. Also, frequent breaks can reduce burnout for both students and teachers.

students.name: Emily
students.text: Although I share Ben's view that learning loss is a concern, I think the traditional calendar is better. Summer provides unique opportunities for camps, family travel, and unstructured play that are essential for development. Year-round schedules can complicate childcare and family vacations. Instead, we should improve summer programs rather than overhaul the system.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: We've been studying different approaches to reducing plastic waste, and I came across an interesting case: several countries have banned single-use plastic bags, but critics argue that paper or cotton bags also have environmental costs. Do you think banning single-use plastics is an effective policy for protecting the environment?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think banning single-use plastics is a great idea. Plastic pollution is a huge problem—it clogs our oceans and harms wildlife. Alternatives like paper bags are biodegradable, and cotton bags can be reused many times. Even if they have some environmental cost, it's much lower than the long-term damage plastics cause. My city banned plastic bags last year, and I've seen people adapt quickly by bringing their own reusable bags.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I'm not convinced that a ban is the best approach. For one thing, paper bags require a lot of water and energy to produce, and cotton bags have a huge carbon footprint unless you use them hundreds of times. Also, many people forget their reusable bags and end up buying new ones, which defeats the purpose. I think a better solution is to improve recycling systems and invest in biodegradable plastics rather than outright bans.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: We've been discussing how companies can attract and retain top talent. Some argue that offering high salaries is the most effective way, while others believe that factors like company culture, work-life balance, or opportunities for growth are more important. What do you think is the most significant factor in attracting talented employees? Why?

students.name: Ronald
students.text: I believe salary is still the most important factor. In my experience, people work primarily to earn a living, and a competitive salary shows that the company values their skills. For example, my cousin turned down a job with great culture because the pay was too low. High salary also signals prestige and can motivate employees to perform better.

students.name: Olivia
students.text: While Ronald makes a good point about salary, I think company culture and growth opportunities are more crucial in the long run. A high salary might attract someone initially, but if the work environment is toxic or there's no room for advancement, talented employees will leave. I've seen friends quit well-paying jobs because they felt undervalued or bored. So, culture and development are key for retention.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about the impact of community-based programs on reducing crime. Some cities have implemented initiatives like neighborhood watch programs, youth mentorship schemes, and community policing. Which approach do you think is most effective in preventing crime and why?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe youth mentorship programs are the most effective. When young people have positive role models, they are less likely to engage in criminal activities. In my hometown, a mentoring program paired at-risk teens with local professionals. Over five years, the juvenile crime rate dropped by 30%. These programs also address root causes like poverty and lack of opportunity.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I think community policing is the best method. When officers build trust with residents, people are more willing to report crimes and cooperate with investigations. In a city near me, community policing led to a 25% decrease in violent crime within two years. It also reduces fear of the police, especially in minority neighborhoods.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: A growing number of cities are implementing 'universal basic mobility' programs, providing residents with free or subsidized access to public transit, bike-sharing, and ride-sharing services. Proponents argue this reduces inequality and environmental harm. However, critics say it's costly and may not address the root causes of car dependence. What's your take on universal basic mobility? Should cities invest in such programs?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think universal basic mobility is a great idea. In my city, public transit is expensive and unreliable, so many people rely on cars. If the city provided free bus passes and bike-sharing, it would help low-income residents get to work and school more easily. Plus, it could reduce traffic and pollution. I've seen similar programs in Europe that work well.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I'm not convinced. While the goal is admirable, these programs are very expensive to run and maintain. The money could be better spent on improving existing infrastructure, like building more bike lanes or making streets safer for pedestrians. Also, free transit might not reduce car use if the service is still slow or inconvenient. We need to address the underlying reasons people drive.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: We often assume that our memories are accurate records of past events. However, research in psychology shows that memory is not like a video recording; it is reconstructed each time we recall it. This means memories can change over time, influenced by new information or suggestions. Do you think it's possible to have a truly accurate memory of an event from years ago?

students.name: Lena
students.text: I believe it's very difficult to have a perfectly accurate memory of a distant event. I remember a family vacation from when I was eight, but when I look at photos, I realize some details are wrong. For instance, I thought we went to the beach on a sunny day, but the photos show it was cloudy. Our brains fill in gaps with plausible details, making memories unreliable.

students.name: Emily
students.text: I disagree. Some memories are extremely vivid and can be accurate, especially if the event was emotionally significant. I can recall my high school graduation ceremony in great detail, and my parents confirm most of what I remember. While some details may fade, the core of the memory can remain true. It's not all unreliable.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: We often hear that society places a high value on youth and beauty, and that aging is viewed negatively. But is that always the case? In some cultures, older adults are respected for their wisdom and experience. What do you think is the most significant social consequence of society's emphasis on youth? Why?

students.name: Steve
students.text: I think the most significant consequence is that older people are often marginalized in the workplace and in social life. For example, many companies prefer to hire younger workers, assuming they are more energetic and adaptable. This can lead to age discrimination and financial insecurity for older adults. Also, media constantly promotes anti-aging products and youthful ideals, which can make older people feel invisible or less valuable.

students.name: Aisha
students.text: In my opinion, the biggest social impact is the pressure it puts on everyone, not just older people. Society's focus on youth creates a fear of aging that starts early. People spend huge amounts of money on cosmetic procedures and products to look younger, and this can lead to anxiety and low self-esteem. Additionally, it reinforces the idea that appearance is more important than character or achievement, which is a harmful message for all ages.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about employee motivation. Many companies use financial incentives like bonuses and raises to motivate their workers. However, some research suggests that non-financial factors, such as recognition, autonomy, and meaningful work, may be more effective in the long run. Which factor do you think is most effective for motivating employees and why?

students.name: Tim
students.text: I believe financial incentives are the most effective motivator. Money is a tangible reward that directly improves people's quality of life. In my own experience, when my previous employer offered a performance-based bonus, I worked much harder to achieve my targets. Plus, bonuses and raises are easy to implement and measure, making them a straightforward tool for management.

students.name: John
students.text: I think non-financial factors like recognition and autonomy are more powerful. While money is important, it often leads to only short-term motivation. When employees feel valued and have control over their work, they are more engaged and creative. For example, my friend works at a company that gives employees freedom to choose their projects, and she says she's much more satisfied and productive than when she had a higher-paying job with strict oversight.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: We often assume that our memories are accurate records of past events, but research in cognitive psychology suggests that memory is highly reconstructive and prone to errors. For example, eyewitness testimony can be influenced by leading questions or post-event information. Considering this, do you think that relying on memory in legal settings is too risky? Should we place less weight on eyewitness accounts?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think memory is too unreliable to be the sole basis for a conviction. There have been many cases where people were wrongly convicted based on faulty eyewitness testimony. For instance, the Innocence Project has exonerated hundreds of prisoners using DNA evidence, and many of those cases involved mistaken identifications. I believe that while memory can be useful, it should be corroborated with other evidence, and juries should be educated about its fallibility.

students.name: Paul
students.text: While Claire makes a good point about the fallibility of memory, I think we shouldn't dismiss eyewitness testimony entirely. In many cases, it is the only evidence available. Instead of reducing its weight, we should improve how we collect it, such as using double-blind lineups and interviewing techniques that minimize suggestion. Also, some memories are quite vivid and accurate, especially for traumatic events. So I believe memory can still be valuable if handled properly.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about vaccination policies in public schools. Many districts now require students to be fully immunized before enrolling, with exemptions only for medical reasons. Critics say this overrides parental choice and personal beliefs, while supporters argue it's the only reliable way to prevent outbreaks of diseases like measles. Before our next class, I'd like you to weigh in: should public schools strictly enforce vaccination requirements, or should families have broader rights to opt out?

students.name: Diane
students.text: I think schools have to enforce strict vaccination rules. When my younger sister was in kindergarten, an unvaccinated kid brought measles into her class and three children ended up hospitalized. Honestly, it scared everyone in the neighborhood. Schools aren't private spaces, they're shared environments, and one family's choice can put hundreds of other kids at real risk. If we allow broad opt-outs, we're basically asking the most vulnerable students, like infants in the same household or kids on chemo, to absorb the danger. That doesn't feel fair to me.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I see Diane's point about outbreaks, but I'm worried about how strict enforcement actually plays out. A family I babysat for moved here from another country and had paperwork in a different format, and the school basically locked their kid out of class for two months while they sorted it. There's a difference between requiring vaccines and creating a system where any administrative slip kicks a kid out of school. I'd rather see flexible enforcement, with clear appeal paths, instead of treating every missing form like a public health emergency.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we've discussed in class, governments around the world are debating whether public agencies should rely mainly on open-source software or stick with proprietary tools from large vendors. Open-source supporters argue that public code is more transparent, easier to audit, and frees agencies from vendor lock-in. Proprietary defenders point to professional support, security guarantees, and faster updates. For public institutions handling tax records, health data, and elections, which approach do you think is the better default?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: I'd push for open source as the default. My city's transit agency switched from a closed scheduling system to an open one two years ago, and within a semester, local computer science students were submitting fixes that the original vendor had ignored for years. When the code is public, you get accountability that no service contract can buy. Sure, proprietary software has glossy support lines, but when something breaks at 2 a.m., having engineers anywhere in the world able to read your stack is a real advantage.

students.name: Ross
students.text: Open source sounds great in theory, but I worked at a hospital IT department over the summer, and reality is messier. The nurses didn't want philosophical debates about software freedom, they wanted a system that just worked when a patient came in bleeding. The proprietary vendor had a 24/7 hotline, certified training, and someone to legally blame when things went wrong. Most public agencies don't have in-house developers who can read source code anyway, so 'transparency' becomes a feature that nobody actually uses.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Several major cities have recently introduced congestion pricing, charging drivers a fee to enter the busiest downtown areas during peak hours. London has done this for two decades, and New York started a version last year. Supporters say it cuts traffic, lowers emissions, and funds public transit. Critics argue it disproportionately burdens working-class commuters and small business owners who depend on driving in. Do you think congestion pricing is a fair tool for managing urban traffic, or does the harm to lower-income drivers outweigh the benefits?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I support congestion pricing, even though it isn't perfect. The bus I take to campus used to crawl through downtown for almost forty minutes during rush hour. After a pilot fee was introduced, my commute dropped to twenty-two minutes, and the fees were earmarked for a new bus lane on my route. People act like the only choice is 'fee or no fee,' but really it's 'fee, or everyone stuck in traffic burning gas.' Pair the pricing with discounts for low-income drivers and you address most fairness concerns.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I'm with Claire on traffic, but not on framing this as fair. My uncle delivers produce to small restaurants downtown, and a daily fee plus inflation already pushes some of his clients out of business. Office workers can switch to the train, but plumbers, electricians, and food suppliers can't carry their tools on a subway. Before charging everyone the same fee, cities should exempt service vehicles and small commercial operators. Otherwise, congestion pricing just shifts costs onto the people who keep the city actually running.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Most major news platforms now rely on algorithms to decide which stories each user sees on their homepage. This personalization keeps people engaged, but it has also been blamed for filter bubbles and a narrowing range of perspectives. Some argue that platforms should offer a 'chronological-only' option as a default, while others say algorithmic curation is the only way to handle the flood of daily news. For our discussion this week, should news platforms be required to offer an algorithm-free feed as a standard choice?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: Yes, an algorithm-free option should be a standard, not a buried setting. Last semester I tried switching my main news app to a chronological feed for a research paper, and within a week I was seeing stories from local councils, smaller outlets, and writers the algorithm had quietly hidden for months. Most users won't dig through five menus to find that setting. Making it a clear default doesn't ban personalization, it just gives people a fair off-ramp when they realize their feed has been steering them somewhere specific.

students.name: Tim
students.text: I'm skeptical that defaults can fix this. A friend of mine works at a news startup, and she said the few users who switched to chronological mode mostly stopped reading the app altogether because the volume felt overwhelming. The bigger problem isn't the algorithm itself, it's that most readers want curation and just don't trust who is doing it. I'd rather see rules forcing platforms to disclose how stories get ranked, with audits by independent researchers, instead of pretending a chronological feed is some neutral solution.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: How should computer science programs respond to AI coding assistants like GitHub Copilot? Some instructors are banning these tools in introductory classes, arguing that students need to struggle through writing their own loops and debugging their own errors to truly learn. Others welcome the tools, claiming professional programmers already rely on them daily. Before our next class, share your view: should we restrict AI coding assistants in intro-level CS courses?

students.name: David
students.text: Honestly, intro courses exist to build the foundation. When I took CS1 last year, the most useful thing I learned wasn't writing perfect code — it was getting stuck on a segfault for three hours and finally understanding what was actually happening in memory. If a tool just hands me the answer, I skip the part where my brain rewires itself. Once you have the foundation, sure, use Copilot. But not before then.

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I get the foundation argument, but I think it's outdated. My older brother is a working software engineer and he uses AI tools constantly — pretending students won't need that skill feels disconnected from reality. We should be teaching students to use AI critically: reviewing what it generates, catching its mistakes, asking better questions. That is the modern foundation. Banning the tools just means students learn to use them sloppily later.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about mandatory mental health screenings in the workplace. Several large companies have started offering annual mental health check-ups for all employees as part of regular wellness programs, similar to physical health exams. Supporters say this reduces stigma and catches problems early. Critics worry about employee privacy and the risk that screening results could quietly affect job security. Before our next class, please share your view: should employers be allowed to require routine mental health screenings for all staff?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think mandatory screenings are a good idea, especially given how much we now know about the link between mental health and overall productivity. Regular check-ups normalize the conversation around mental health and make it less awkward to seek help. When my aunt's company started offering screenings, she found out she had been dealing with mild depression for years without realizing it. Privacy concerns can be addressed through strict confidentiality rules. The benefits outweigh the risks.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Claire's example about her aunt is really compelling, and I agree that normalizing mental health conversations matters. But I think MAKING screenings mandatory crosses a line. Employees who refuse may be seen as hiding something, and even with confidentiality rules, leaks happen. Someone diagnosed with anxiety could quietly get passed over for a promotion. I would support voluntary screenings paired with strong protections, but mandatory ones turn a wellness tool into a surveillance tool.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Deepfake technology has improved dramatically in the last two years, and AI-generated videos of public figures saying things they never said are now circulating widely on social media platforms. Some news organizations have started labeling all video content with a 'verified' or 'unverified' tag, while others argue this gives the false impression that anything without a 'verified' tag must be fake. For this week's post: should major news platforms be required to label all video content for authenticity?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: I support mandatory labeling. The cost of doing nothing is too high — once a deepfake of a politician saying something inflammatory goes viral, the damage is done even after it's debunked. People share clips faster than corrections spread. Labels create friction, and friction gives the truth a chance to catch up. Sure, the system isn't perfect, but waiting for a perfect solution before acting is how we ended up with this mess in the first place.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I'm not against labels in principle, but mandating them creates a problem the professor hinted at. Once 'verified' becomes the standard expectation, anything without that label starts to look suspicious — including small independent journalists who don't have the tech budget to authenticate every clip. We end up centralizing trust around big platforms that can afford the verification infrastructure. That's not great for media diversity. I'd rather invest heavily in digital literacy education.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we discussed last week, hundreds of indigenous languages around the world are predicted to disappear within this century. Several universities and museums have launched digital archive projects, recording native speakers and creating online dictionaries before the last fluent speakers pass away. Critics argue that digital preservation is no substitute for living use — a language preserved only in a database is, in a sense, already dead. For our next class, share your perspective: are digital language archives a genuine form of preservation, or just a record of loss?

students.name: Lena
students.text: Digital archives matter, even if the language isn't actively spoken anymore. My grandmother grew up speaking a regional dialect that almost nobody uses now, and last year we found old recordings of her village's stories in a university archive. Hearing those voices changed how I think about my own family history. Even if a language eventually goes silent, having a record means future generations — or even AI tools someday — could potentially revive it. The alternative is just letting it vanish forever.

students.name: Ryan
students.text: Lena's story is moving, but I think it actually proves the opposite point. Recordings of stories her family no longer speaks aren't preservation — they're nostalgia. A language lives by being used: argued in, joked in, taught to a complaining child at dinnertime. None of that happens in a database. I'd rather see funding go to community immersion programs and bilingual schools, where languages can actually keep being living things, even if those programs reach fewer speakers.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Vertical farms — multi-story buildings where crops grow under LED lights in stacked trays — are spreading quickly in cities like Singapore and Seoul. Supporters say they cut transportation emissions, save water through closed-loop irrigation, and put fresh produce within walking distance of consumers. But critics point out that the lights and climate systems often run on fossil-fuel electricity, and the high startup cost limits who actually benefits. Do you think cities should invest public funds in vertical farms?

students.name: Diane
students.text: I live in a neighborhood where the closest grocery store is a 25-minute bus ride away, so I really like the idea of vertical farms. Last summer one opened on top of an old factory near my building, and they sell lettuce, basil, and strawberries for almost the same price as the supermarket. Even if the energy comes from natural gas right now, the system can switch to solar once panels are added. Public funding makes sense because the people who benefit most can't usually afford the high grocery delivery fees.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I see why a place like Diane's neighborhood would love them, but I'm not convinced cities should pay. The LEDs in those buildings draw a huge amount of power, and a recent report from my hometown showed one vertical farm used more electricity in a month than 200 apartments combined. That's a serious problem when most of the grid still burns gas. I'd rather see public money go toward rooftop greenhouses, which use real sunlight and cost much less to maintain. Vertical farms can stay private for now.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about a small but growing trend in city government — paying residents small cash rewards, usually $5 to $20, for reporting infrastructure problems like potholes or broken streetlights through a mobile app. Cities that try this say repairs get done two to three times faster, but skeptics argue that the program turns neighbors into informants and unfairly rewards people with smartphones. Should city governments pay residents for these reports?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think cash rewards are a smart use of public money. My uncle works for a city public works department and he says that until they launched their reporting app, they relied on monthly road surveys that missed half the damaged blocks. Now they fix potholes in about three days instead of three months. The rewards are tiny compared to what a broken-axle lawsuit costs the city, and $5 doesn't really turn anyone into a paid informant. Most reports come from people who'd file them anyway.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I'd be more comfortable if cities just hired more maintenance inspectors. The reward system has a quiet bias — older residents and people without good phones get left out, even though they often live near the worst infrastructure. Plus, what counts as 'reportable' starts to shift over time: in one program I read about, drivers started reporting parked cars they didn't like as 'obstructions.' Once you attach money to reporting your neighbors, the line gets messy.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Should every senior capstone project in a computer science program be released under an open-source license by default? Universities like Stanford and the University of Toronto already require this, arguing that students learn more from peer review and that society benefits when academic code is freely available. Other programs push back, noting that students who later try to commercialize their project face real difficulty raising investment if the code is already public. Which side do you think is right?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: I would have learned so much faster if our capstone code had been open by default.
